content.text: CSS Grid is a two-dimensional layout system that lets you position elements in both rows and columns simultaneously. Unlike Flexbox, which is inherently one-dimensional, Grid gives you precise control over both axes at once — making it the right tool for page-level layouts, complex component grids, and any design where rows and columns need to align across the full structure.

attrs.alt: CSS Grid key concepts: template, placement, named areas, fr unit, and alignment

content.text: Defining a Grid: grid-template-columns and grid-template-rows

means the second column gets twice as much of the remaining space.
Track clamped between min and max

places empty tracks at the end, which matters for alignment.
collapses empty tracks so items stretch to fill the container. Use
Grid items can be placed explicitly by specifying the start and end grid lines. Lines are numbered from 1 on the left/top, and -1 from the right/bottom.
shorthand combines row-start / column-start / row-end / column-end:
Named areas make layouts self-documenting. You define a visual map in the container and reference area names in items:
in the area map means an empty cell. Rectangular areas only — you cannot create L-shaped areas with this syntax.
) sets spacing between tracks. It does not add space outside the grid container.
Grid has four alignment properties. The terminology follows the CSS Box Alignment spec:
When items are placed outside the explicit grid (e.g. more items than defined rows), the browser creates implicit tracks. Control their size with
controls how the auto-placement algorithm fills the grid. The default is
backfills gaps when later items are smaller:

(supported in all modern browsers since 2023) lets a nested grid participate in its parent's track sizes. This solves the classic problem of aligning labels and inputs inside cards that themselves sit in a grid:
Grid works perfectly with Angular's component model. The grid container is typically the component host or a wrapper element:
Grid vs Flexbox — When to Use Each
Navigation links on a single axis
Table-like data with alignment across rows
Single dynamic list of varying-size items
Grid and Flexbox complement each other. Use Grid for the page structure and Flexbox for the layout within each grid cell.
Setting `height: 100%` on a grid item inside a `min-height` container.
Height percentages need an explicit parent height. Use

`grid-column: span N` placing items outside the explicit grid.
If you span more columns than defined, implicit columns are created. Double-check your
) is more maintainable than placing every item manually.
In older environments that don't support a particular grid feature, the layout may collapse unexpectedly. Test progressively.
One of the most powerful Grid patterns is the intrinsically responsive grid — it adapts to any container width using

This creates as many 260px-minimum columns as will fit. When the container narrows, columns wrap to the next row automatically. The result is a fluid grid that shows 4 columns on a desktop, 2 on a tablet, and 1 on mobile — without any breakpoints.
for card grids where items should fill available width. Use
when you need consistent column widths and empty slots (e.g. calendars, planning boards).
You can name grid lines to make placement code more readable:
Named lines are especially useful for magazine-style layouts where some elements bleed to the container edge and others stay within a content column.
Here is a complete Angular dashboard layout using Grid with named areas and responsive column collapse:

// dashboard.component.ts
@Component({
  selector: 'app-dashboard',
  template: `
    <nav class="sidebar">
      <app-nav-links />
    </nav>
    <header class="topbar">
      <app-topbar />
    </header>
    <main class="content">
      <router-outlet />
    </main>
  `,
  styleUrls: ['./dashboard.component.scss']
})
export class DashboardComponent {}
A reusable card grid that accepts items as content projection and adapts from 1 to 4 columns:

<!-- Usage -->
<app-card-grid style="--card-min-width: 220px; --card-gap: 16px;">
  @for (product of products(); track product.id) {
    <app-product-card [product]="product" />
  }
</app-card-grid>

. However, certain sizing patterns can lead to overflow:

Modern browser DevTools have first-class Grid debugging support:
In Chrome/Firefox DevTools, select the grid container and click the
badge to overlay the grid lines.
(Firefox) to toggle line numbers, area names, and track sizes.
in the console to inspect the component and check bound inputs.
This CSS utility overlays a 12-column grid guide as a background image, useful during development without opening DevTools.
